name: Witherington
overview: Witherington is a small ancient settlement and tithing in the civil parish of Downton, in the extreme south-east of Wiltshire, on rising ground near the east bank of the River Avon about 4-5 miles south-east of Salisbury. Although surveyed in the Domesday Book, it is today little more than Witherington Farm, close to Trafalgar Park, Standlynch and Charlton-All-Saints. The site is now best known locally for Witherington Farm Fishery, a set of coarse-fishing lakes.
character: Essentially a single working farm and fishery in quiet Avon-valley countryside, with water meadows, gravel terraces and downland all around and no village around it.
amenities: No shops, pub or services - effectively a single farm
Nearest full range of amenities in Downton (~2-3 miles): schools, shops, pubs, leisure centre and a local brewery

commutes.notes: About 4-5 miles, via the A338 Avon-valley road
From Salisbury station; Downton's own station closed in 1964

prices.summary: Effectively a single farm/estate holding, so there is almost no open-market housing here and sales are extremely rare. For any local context use the wider SP5 / Downton-area sold-price record.

thingsToDo: Coarse fishing at Witherington Farm Fishery
Trafalgar Park (Nelson's former estate) close by
The New Forest National Park within easy reach
pros: Close to Downton's amenities and to Salisbury, with the New Forest nearby
cons: Effectively a single farm with no village or amenities
whoItSuits: Those wanting a very private rural or agricultural setting in the Avon valley, within easy reach of Downton and Salisbury.

sources.label: VCH Wiltshire vol. 11: Downton - British History Online
